content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
Lance Olson 
Olson Hagel & Fishburn 
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1425 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
April 1, 2014 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-14-047 
Dear Mr. Olson: 
This letter responds to your request for advice, on behalf of Dr. Gerald Meral, regarding 
the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter should not be 
construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place. (See Regulation 
18329(b)(8)(A).) In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it provides 
advice. (/11 re Oglesby (1975) l FPPC Ops. 71.) We offer no opinion on the application of laws 
other than the Political Reform Act, such as the post-employment provisions of Public Contract 
Code Section I 0411. Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your request 
as one for informal assistance. 2 
QUESTIONS 
I) As the former Deputy Secretary of the California Natural Resources Agency, what 
restrictions do the Act's revolving door provisions have on Dr. Meral's private employment with 
the Natural Heritage Institute? 
2) Do the Act's revolving door provisions apply if Dr. Meral acts as an "unpaid 
volunteer" on behalf of his employer while simultaneously being compensated for any services 
that are not prohibited? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9!014. All statutory 
references arc to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
wriuen advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. 1-14-047 
Page No. 2 
1) Under the one-year ban, Dr. Meral is prohibited as a paid employee from making an 
appearance or communication before his former agency, or any agency under the direction and 
control of his former agency, to influence any administrative or legislative action and any action 
or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property for 12 months after leaving 
governmental employment. Dr. Mera] is also prohibited under the permanent ban from making 
an appearance or communication on behalf of his private employer, or from assisting his 
employer in making an appearance or communication before any state administrative agency for 
the purpose of influencing a proceeding involving a specific party or parties if he previously 
participated in the proceeding as a state official. 
2) For purposes of the revolving door provisions, Dr. Meral is representing his private 
employer for compensation so long as he is a paid employee. Dr. Meral may not provide 
"volunteer" services on behalf of his employer, which would otherwise be prohibited, while 
simultaneously being compensated for other services. 
FACTS 
Dr. Gerald Meral served as the Deputy Secretary for the California Natural Resources 
Agency (the "CNRA") from 20 I l to December 31, 2013. As the Deputy Secretary, Dr. Meral 
was primarily responsible for managing the Bay Delta Conservation Plan (the "BDCP"), serving 
as the administration's public face of the planning process, leading public meetings and giving 
presentations around the state. He also worked on flow issues regarding the San Joaquin River. 
The CNRA is responsible for protecting historical, natural and cultural sites, monitoring 
and controlling state lands and waterways, and regulating fish and game use. Additionally, the 
CNRA oversees 25 departments, conservancies and commissions, including the Department of 
Fish and Wildlife (the "DFW") and the Department of Water Resources (the "DWR"). 
The BDCP is a 50-year habitat conservation plan with the goals of restoring the 
Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta ecosystem and securing California water supplies through 
specific actions, called conservation measures. The BDCP is subject to environmental review 
under both the National Environmental Policy Act and the California Environmental Quality 
Act. After the public review and comment period, the final environmental impact 
report/statement for the plan will be submitted to state and federal regulatory agencies for 
approval. If approved, the DWR must then apply for long-term permits from the DFW, the U.S. 
Fish and Wildlife Service, and the National Marine Fisheries Service. If granted, the DWR 
would then apply for permits from the State Water Resources Control Board (the "WRCB"), 
which is part of the California Environmental Protection Agency, and the U.S. Army Corps of 
Engineers. The final decisions regarding going forward with the BDCP or in selecting an 
alternative plan will only occur after the completion of this environmental review. 
\ 


File No. 1-14-047 
Page No. 3 
You state that during his tenure with the CNRA, Dr. Mera! was not involved in any 
policy issues, legislative actions, contract decisions, or the regulatory process, nor did he 
participate in making governmental decisions in relation to any areas outside of the BDCP 
planning process and flow issues on the San Joaquin River. After retiring from state service, Dr. 
Meral joined the Natural Heritage Institute (the "NHI") to direct its California water program. 
The NHI is a non-governmental, non-profit environmental organization. Its core mission is to 
restore and protect the natural functions that support water-dependent ecosystems and the 
services they provide to sustain and enrich human life. In fulfilling its mission, the NHI acts as 
both a representative of environmental interests and a counselor to the ultimate custodians, 
managers and regulators of water resource assets. The NHI also represents conservation interests 
in complex, multi-party negotiations over the allocation of natural resources and works with 
governmental and non-governmental organizations to shape policy and engage policy-makers. 
Dr. Meral intends to provide both paid and "volunteer" services to the NHI. In those 
instances in which his services are prohibited under the Act's revolving door rules, Dr. Mera! 
would like to provide his services as an "unpaid volunteer" with only reimbursement for travel, 
subsistence, and accommodations made in connection with the services. Notwithstanding any 
volunteer services, Dr. Meral will be paid hourly for all other work performed on the NHrs 
behalf. Paid activities will involve advising and representing NHI and its clients and 
collaborators on a number of matters unrelated to the BDCP and the San Joaquin River, 
including ( 1) a groundwater study that would likely involve interactions with the WRCB, an 
agency not under the CNRA's oversight; (2) assisting with the Highway 37 expansion project 
and appearing before the DFW after the conclusion of the one-year ban to influence decisions 
regarding the project; and (3) a study of reservoir reoperation in the Central Valley conducted as 
part of the NHI' s contract with the DWR to investigate the feasibility of re-operating Central 
Valley reservoirs. 
ANALYSIS 
Under the Act, public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post­
governmental employment provisions known as the one-year and permanent bans. In addition, 
Section 87407 prohibits certain state and local officials from making, participating in making, or 
using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are 
negotiating employment or have any arrangement concerning employment. (See Regulation 
18747.)3 Colloquially, these provisions are known as the "revo]ving door" prohibitions. 
One-Year Ban 
The one-year ban prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her 
3 Because Dr. Mcral has already left the CNRA, we are not providing advice pertaining 10 Section 87407. 


File No. I-14-047 
Page No. 4 
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action4 or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, 
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; 
Regulation 18746.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
(Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746.l(a)(2).)5 The ban applies for 12 months from the date 
the employee permanently leaves state office or employment.6 
In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to certain 'judicial or quasi-judicial" 
proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the 
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or 
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 18746.1 (b )(5).) An 
appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the "principal 
purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the 
action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication includes 
conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission, 
attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Id.) 
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited if they are (I) before a state 
agency that the public official worked for or represented or (2) before a state agency "which 
budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public 
official worked for or represented. (Regulation 18746. l(b)(6).) 
4 For purposes of Section 87406, the Act defines "administrative action" and "legislative action" as the 
following: 
'''Administrative action' means the proposal, drafting, development, consideration, 
amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation, or other action in 
any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi•legislative proceeding .... " (Section 82002(a).) 
, .. Legislative action' means the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification, 
enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the 
Legislature or by either house or any commiuec, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, 
or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. 'Legislative action' 
also means the action of the Governor in approving or vetoing any bill." (Section 82037.) 
5 A governmental employee should be designated in his or her agency's conflict-of-interest code if the 
employee makes or participates in making governmental decisions that have a reasonably foreseeable material effect 
on any financial interest. (Section 87302.) 
6 For purposes of the one-year ban, the date on which an official permanently leaves office or employment 
is the dale on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the duties of that office or employment, and stops 
performing those duties. A person shall not be deemed to have left office permanently because he or she is on a 
leave of absence or serves as an intermittent employee. However, a person shall be deemed to have left office 
permanently if the person merely receives compensation for accrued leave credits. (Regulation 18746.4(b).) 


File No. 1-14-047 
Page No. 5 
As the Deputy Secretary, Dr. Meral's former position is or should be designated in the 
DHCS's conflict-of-interest code, and his post-employment actions as an employee of the NHI 
are restricted under the one-year ban for 12 months from the date he permanently left the agency. 
Because the one-year ban applies to any administrative or legislative action and any action or 
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property, the ban prohibits Dr. Mera) from 
representing the NHI in any administrative proceedings before the CNRA, such as general 
decisions regarding the BDCP. 
Additionally, the one-year ban applies to appearance or communications before any 
agency "whose budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control" 
of the official's former agency. (Regulation 18746. l(b)(6).) For Dr. Meral's purposes, the one­
year ban also applies to appearances or communications before the DFW and the DWR, which 
are both under the CNRA's authority. The one-year ban does not apply to decisions before the 
WRCB, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, the National Marine Fisheries Service, or the U.S. 
Army Corps of Engineers. However, in taking part in a decision before these agencies, Dr. 
Meral may not use the occasion to make a prohibited communication to those agencies that are 
covered by the ban. (See Chan Advice Letter, No. 1-09-063.) 
Addressing the projects you have specifically identified, the one-year ban does not 
prohibit Dr. Meral from making an appearance or communication before the WRCB regarding 
the groundwater study, but does prohibit an appearance or communication before the DFW 
regarding the Highway 37 expansion project. For the reservoir reoperation study, the one-year 
ban prohibits Dr. Meral from making an appearance or communication for the purpose of 
influencing certain actions involving the NHJ's contract with the DWR, such as an amendment 
of the contract, but does not prohibit him from making an appearance or communication for the 
purpose of implementing the existing contract. Pursuant to Regulation 18746.l(c), appearances 
or communications made as part of "[s]ervices performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the 
requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or sale agreement are excluded from 
the [one-year prohibitions] ... provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, 
awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings." (See also Quiring Advice 
Letter, No. A-03-272, and Hanan Advice Letter, No. 1-00•209.) 
Pennanelll Ban 
The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in any specific proceeding involving the State of California or 
assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee 
participated while employed by the state. (Sections 8740 l and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) The 
permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or ta1ces a leave of absence from any 
particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741.1 (a)( 1 )} 
7 For purposes of !he permanenl ban, "[!)he dale on which an official permanently leaves office or 
employment or lakes a leave of absence is !he dale on which 1he official is no longer aulhorizcd 10 perform 1he 


File No. 1-14-047 
Page No. 6 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or 
any oral or written communication, or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication, made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other 
proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
'"Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency .... " (Section 87400(c).) An official is considered to have "participated" 
in a proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through 
decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a 
substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information .... " (Section 87400(d).) 
"The permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, a 
different subject matter, or different factual issues from those considered in previous 
proceedings." (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; see also Do11ova11 Advice Letter, No. 1-03-
119.) New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not 
participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. 1-89-649.) A new 
contract is one that is based on new consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same 
parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. 1-99-104; A11derso11 Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In 
addition, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered 
to be a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or 
approval. (A11derso11, supra; B/011ie11 Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.) 
As the former Deputy Secretary for the CNRA, Dr. Meral is a former state employee 
subject to the permanent ban. Accordingly, he is prohibited from making an appearance or 
communication, or from assisting any other person including the NHI, in making an appearance 
or communication, before any state administrative agency, for compensation, in a quasi-judicial 
proceeding if he previously participated in the proceeding as a state official. 
While you have identified several proceedings in which Dr. Meral would like to 
participate that are unrelated to the BDCP or the San Joaquin River, you have stated that Dr. 
Meral "was not involved in any policy issues, legislative actions, contract decisions, or the 
regulatory process, nor did he participate in making governmental decisions in relation to any 
areas outside of the BDCP planning process and flow issues on the San Joaquin River." To the 
extent that the Dr. Meral did not participate in the proceeding as a state employee, the permanent 
ban does not apply. Moreover, in regard to proceedings involving the BDCP or the San Joaquin 
River, the permanent ban only applies to proceedings involving a specific party or parties. The 
permanent ban does not apply to general administrative proceedings involving the BDCP or the 
duties of the office or employment, and the official stops performing those duties, even if the official continues to 
receive compensation for accrued leave credits." (Regulation l 8746.4(a)( I).) 


File No. 1-14-047 
Page No. 7 
San Joaquin River. If Dr. Mera) has any additional questions regarding the application of the 
permanent ban to any particular proceeding, he should seek further advice describing the nature 
of the proceeding and his past involvement. 
Mav Dr. Mera/ provide "volunteer" services 011 behalf of his emplover that are othenvise 
prohibited under the revolving door provisions while being compensated for other services? 
In your request for assistance, you have suggested that Dr. Meral would "voluntarily" 
represent the NHI and forego compensation in any proceeding in which he would be prohibited 
from representing the NHI under the Act's revolving door provisions. However, Dr. Mera) 
would continue to receive compensation for any other work he performs on the NHI's behalf. 
Accordingly, we must determine whether the compensation he receives for other purposes is 
sufficient to disqualify him from those proceeding in which he is technically representing the 
NHI voluntarily. 
Previously, we have advised that the term "compensation" is broadly defined for 
purposes of the revolving door provisions and includes remuneration or payment of any kind, 
whether received contemporaneously with the services rendered, at a future date, or even if it is 
paid by a third party. (Souza, A-06-114.) Under the facts submitted, we simply cannot ignore 
the fact that Dr. Mera) is receiving compensation to provide services on the NHI's behalf. Even 
if technically volunteering some of his services, it seems illogical to separate the pay he is 
receiving to represent the NHI from volunteer services performed on the NHI's behalf. 
Moreover, we do not believe the Act's revolving door provisions intended to create a loophole 
whereby former officials could simply go off their employer's clock for an afternoon, appear 
before their former agency for the purpose of engaging in otherwise prohibited communications, 
and then simply go back on the clock when finished. 
Rather, we interpret the compensation provision of the ban to cover situations wherein, 
for example, a former official simply volunteers time to represent a party with which he or she 
has no other compensation arrangement for similar types of services. We believe such an 
application of these provisions of the Act assists us in fulfilling our obligation to liberally 
construe the Act to accomplish its purposes (see Section 81003). The revolving door provisions 
were added by legislation sponsored by the Commission in 1979. 
"That legislation, AB 1048 (Waters), was drafted by the Commission to 
embody the following principles: 
'1 The interests of the government and the public as a former client 
should be protected by preventing the disclosure or use of matters revealed by 
reason of a relationship having confidential aspects. 
'2 The integrity of the governmental decision making process should be 
protected by insuring avoidance of the appearance of impropriety. 


File No. 1-14-047 
Page No. 8 
•3 Both the interests of the government and the public and the integrity of 
the governmental decision making process should be protected by limitations on 
the opportunity for undue advantage by reason of familiarity with agency practice 
and procedure and personal relationships with agency personnel." (In re Lucas, 
14 FPPC Ops. 14, 0-00-157 (2000).) 
Therefore, as an otherwise paid employee for similar types of services, we find Dr. Meral 
is representing his employer for compensation even when technically volunteering his services on 
behalf of his employer only while appearing before his former agency. For that reason, Dr. 
Meral may not "voluntarily" provide services on behalf of his employer that are otherwise 
prohibited under the Act's revolving door provisions while receiving compensation for other 
services. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
d? > 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
I) As the former Deputy Secretary of the California Natural Resources Agency, what 
restrictions do the Act's revolving door provisions have on Dr. Meral's private employment with 
the Natural Heritage Institute? 
2) Do the Act's revolving door provisions apply if Dr. Meral acts as an "unpaid 
volunteer" on behalf of his employer while simultaneously being compensated for any services 
that are not prohibited? 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. 2. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
wriuen advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
sections.conclusion:
1) Under the one-year ban, Dr. Meral is prohibited as a paid employee from making an 
appearance or communication before his former agency, or any agency under the direction and 
control of his former agency, to influence any administrative or legislative action and any action 
or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property for 12 months after leaving 
governmental employment. Dr. Mera] is also prohibited under the permanent ban from making 
an appearance or communication on behalf of his private employer, or from assisting his 
employer in making an appearance or communication before any state administrative agency for 
the purpose of influencing a proceeding involving a specific party or parties if he previously 
participated in the proceeding as a state official. 
2) For purposes of the revolving door provisions, Dr. Meral is representing his private 
employer for compensation so long as he is a paid employee. Dr. Meral may not provide 
"volunteer" services on behalf of his employer, which would otherwise be prohibited, while 
simultaneously being compensated for other services.
sections.facts:
Dr. Gerald Meral served as the Deputy Secretary for the California Natural Resources 
Agency (the "CNRA") from 20 I l to December 31, 2013. As the Deputy Secretary, Dr. Meral 
was primarily responsible for managing the Bay Delta Conservation Plan (the "BDCP"), serving 
as the administration's public face of the planning process, leading public meetings and giving 
presentations around the state. He also worked on flow issues regarding the San Joaquin River. 
The CNRA is responsible for protecting historical, natural and cultural sites, monitoring 
and controlling state lands and waterways, and regulating fish and game use. Additionally, the 
CNRA oversees 25 departments, conservancies and commissions, including the Department of 
Fish and Wildlife (the "DFW") and the Department of Water Resources (the "DWR"). 
The BDCP is a 50-year habitat conservation plan with the goals of restoring the 
Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta ecosystem and securing California water supplies through 
specific actions, called conservation measures. The BDCP is subject to environmental review 
under both the National Environmental Policy Act and the California Environmental Quality 
Act. After the public review and comment period, the final environmental impact 
report/statement for the plan will be submitted to state and federal regulatory agencies for 
approval. If approved, the DWR must then apply for long-term permits from the DFW, the U.S. 
Fish and Wildlife Service, and the National Marine Fisheries Service. If granted, the DWR 
would then apply for permits from the State Water Resources Control Board (the "WRCB"), 
which is part of the California Environmental Protection Agency, and the U.S. Army Corps of 
Engineers. The final decisions regarding going forward with the BDCP or in selecting an 
alternative plan will only occur after the completion of this environmental review. 
\  
You state that during his tenure with the CNRA, Dr. Mera! was not involved in any 
policy issues, legislative actions, contract decisions, or the regulatory process, nor did he 
participate in making governmental decisions in relation to any areas outside of the BDCP 
planning process and flow issues on the San Joaquin River. After retiring from state service, Dr. 
Meral joined the Natural Heritage Institute (the "NHI") to direct its California water program. 
The NHI is a non-governmental, non-profit environmental organization. Its core mission is to 
restore and protect the natural functions that support water-dependent ecosystems and the 
services they provide to sustain and enrich human life. In fulfilling its mission, the NHI acts as 
both a representative of environmental interests and a counselor to the ultimate custodians, 
managers and regulators of water resource assets. The NHI also represents conservation interests 
in complex, multi-party negotiations over the allocation of natural resources and works with 
governmental and non-governmental organizations to shape policy and engage policy-makers. 
Dr. Meral intends to provide both paid and "volunteer" services to the NHI. In those 
instances in which his services are prohibited under the Act's revolving door rules, Dr. Mera! 
would like to provide his services as an "unpaid volunteer" with only reimbursement for travel, 
subsistence, and accommodations made in connection with the services. Notwithstanding any 
volunteer services, Dr. Meral will be paid hourly for all other work performed on the NHrs 
behalf. Paid activities will involve advising and representing NHI and its clients and 
collaborators on a number of matters unrelated to the BDCP and the San Joaquin River, 
including ( 1) a groundwater study that would likely involve interactions with the WRCB, an 
agency not under the CNRA's oversight; (2) assisting with the Highway 37 expansion project 
and appearing before the DFW after the conclusion of the one-year ban to influence decisions 
regarding the project; and (3) a study of reservoir reoperation in the Central Valley conducted as 
part of the NHI' s contract with the DWR to investigate the feasibility of re-operating Central 
Valley reservoirs.
sections.analysis:
Under the Act, public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post­
governmental employment provisions known as the one-year and permanent bans. In addition, 
Section 87407 prohibits certain state and local officials from making, participating in making, or 
using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are 
negotiating employment or have any arrangement concerning employment. (See Regulation 
18747.)3 Colloquially, these provisions are known as the "revo]ving door" prohibitions. 
One-Year Ban 
The one-year ban prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her 
3 Because Dr. Mcral has already left the CNRA, we are not providing advice pertaining 10 Section 87407.  
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action4 or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, 
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; 
Regulation 18746.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
(Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746.l(a)(2).)5 The ban applies for 12 months from the date 
the employee permanently leaves state office or employment.6 
In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to certain 'judicial or quasi-judicial" 
proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the 
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or 
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 18746.1 (b )(5).) An 
appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the "principal 
purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the 
action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication includes 
conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission, 
attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Id.) 
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited if they are (I) before a state 
agency that the public official worked for or represented or (2) before a state agency "which 
budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public 
official worked for or represented. (Regulation 18746. l(b)(6).) 
4 For purposes of Section 87406, the Act defines "administrative action" and "legislative action" as the 
following: 
'''Administrative action' means the proposal, drafting, development, consideration, 
amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation, or other action in 
any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi•legislative proceeding .... " (Section 82002(a).) 
, .. Legislative action' means the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification, 
enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the 
Legislature or by either house or any commiuec, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, 
or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. 'Legislative action' 
also means the action of the Governor in approving or vetoing any bill." (Section 82037.) 
5 A governmental employee should be designated in his or her agency's conflict-of-interest code if the 
employee makes or participates in making governmental decisions that have a reasonably foreseeable material effect 
on any financial interest. (Section 87302.) 
6 For purposes of the one-year ban, the date on which an official permanently leaves office or employment 
is the dale on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the duties of that office or employment, and stops 
performing those duties. A person shall not be deemed to have left office permanently because he or she is on a 
leave of absence or serves as an intermittent employee. However, a person shall be deemed to have left office 
permanently if the person merely receives compensation for accrued leave credits. (Regulation 18746.4(b).)  
As the Deputy Secretary, Dr. Meral's former position is or should be designated in the 
DHCS's conflict-of-interest code, and his post-employment actions as an employee of the NHI 
are restricted under the one-year ban for 12 months from the date he permanently left the agency. 
Because the one-year ban applies to any administrative or legislative action and any action or 
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property, the ban prohibits Dr. Mera) from 
representing the NHI in any administrative proceedings before the CNRA, such as general 
decisions regarding the BDCP. 
Additionally, the one-year ban applies to appearance or communications before any 
agency "whose budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control" 
of the official's former agency. (Regulation 18746. l(b)(6).) For Dr. Meral's purposes, the one­
year ban also applies to appearances or communications before the DFW and the DWR, which 
are both under the CNRA's authority. The one-year ban does not apply to decisions before the 
WRCB, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, the National Marine Fisheries Service, or the U.S. 
Army Corps of Engineers. However, in taking part in a decision before these agencies, Dr. 
Meral may not use the occasion to make a prohibited communication to those agencies that are 
covered by the ban. (See Chan Advice Letter, No. 1-09-063.) 
Addressing the projects you have specifically identified, the one-year ban does not 
prohibit Dr. Meral from making an appearance or communication before the WRCB regarding 
the groundwater study, but does prohibit an appearance or communication before the DFW 
regarding the Highway 37 expansion project. For the reservoir reoperation study, the one-year 
ban prohibits Dr. Meral from making an appearance or communication for the purpose of 
influencing certain actions involving the NHJ's contract with the DWR, such as an amendment 
of the contract, but does not prohibit him from making an appearance or communication for the 
purpose of implementing the existing contract. Pursuant to Regulation 18746.l(c), appearances 
or communications made as part of "[s]ervices performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the 
requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or sale agreement are excluded from 
the [one-year prohibitions] ... provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, 
awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings." (See also Quiring Advice 
Letter, No. A-03-272, and Hanan Advice Letter, No. 1-00•209.) 
Pennanelll Ban 
The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in any specific proceeding involving the State of California or 
assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee 
participated while employed by the state. (Sections 8740 l and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) The 
permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or ta1ces a leave of absence from any 
particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741.1 (a)( 1 )} 
7 For purposes of !he permanenl ban, "[!)he dale on which an official permanently leaves office or 
employment or lakes a leave of absence is !he dale on which 1he official is no longer aulhorizcd 10 perform 1he  
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or 
any oral or written communication, or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication, made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other 
proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
'"Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency .... " (Section 87400(c).) An official is considered to have "participated" 
in a proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through 
decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a 
substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information .... " (Section 87400(d).) 
"The permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, a 
different subject matter, or different factual issues from those considered in previous 
proceedings." (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; see also Do11ova11 Advice Letter, No. 1-03-
119.) New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not 
participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. 1-89-649.) A new 
contract is one that is based on new consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same 
parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. 1-99-104; A11derso11 Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In 
addition, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered 
to be a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or 
approval. (A11derso11, supra; B/011ie11 Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.) 
As the former Deputy Secretary for the CNRA, Dr. Meral is a former state employee 
subject to the permanent ban. Accordingly, he is prohibited from making an appearance or 
communication, or from assisting any other person including the NHI, in making an appearance 
or communication, before any state administrative agency, for compensation, in a quasi-judicial 
proceeding if he previously participated in the proceeding as a state official. 
While you have identified several proceedings in which Dr. Meral would like to 
participate that are unrelated to the BDCP or the San Joaquin River, you have stated that Dr. 
Meral "was not involved in any policy issues, legislative actions, contract decisions, or the 
regulatory process, nor did he participate in making governmental decisions in relation to any 
areas outside of the BDCP planning process and flow issues on the San Joaquin River." To the 
extent that the Dr. Meral did not participate in the proceeding as a state employee, the permanent 
ban does not apply. Moreover, in regard to proceedings involving the BDCP or the San Joaquin 
River, the permanent ban only applies to proceedings involving a specific party or parties. The 
permanent ban does not apply to general administrative proceedings involving the BDCP or the 
duties of the office or employment, and the official stops performing those duties, even if the official continues to 
receive compensation for accrued leave credits." (Regulation l 8746.4(a)( I).)  
San Joaquin River. If Dr. Mera) has any additional questions regarding the application of the 
permanent ban to any particular proceeding, he should seek further advice describing the nature 
of the proceeding and his past involvement. 
Mav Dr. Mera/ provide "volunteer" services 011 behalf of his emplover that are othenvise 
prohibited under the revolving door provisions while being compensated for other services? 
In your request for assistance, you have suggested that Dr. Meral would "voluntarily" 
represent the NHI and forego compensation in any proceeding in which he would be prohibited 
from representing the NHI under the Act's revolving door provisions. However, Dr. Mera) 
would continue to receive compensation for any other work he performs on the NHI's behalf. 
Accordingly, we must determine whether the compensation he receives for other purposes is 
sufficient to disqualify him from those proceeding in which he is technically representing the 
NHI voluntarily. 
Previously, we have advised that the term "compensation" is broadly defined for 
purposes of the revolving door provisions and includes remuneration or payment of any kind, 
whether received contemporaneously with the services rendered, at a future date, or even if it is 
paid by a third party. (Souza, A-06-114.) Under the facts submitted, we simply cannot ignore 
the fact that Dr. Mera) is receiving compensation to provide services on the NHI's behalf. Even 
if technically volunteering some of his services, it seems illogical to separate the pay he is 
receiving to represent the NHI from volunteer services performed on the NHI's behalf. 
Moreover, we do not believe the Act's revolving door provisions intended to create a loophole 
whereby former officials could simply go off their employer's clock for an afternoon, appear 
before their former agency for the purpose of engaging in otherwise prohibited communications, 
and then simply go back on the clock when finished. 
Rather, we interpret the compensation provision of the ban to cover situations wherein, 
for example, a former official simply volunteers time to represent a party with which he or she 
has no other compensation arrangement for similar types of services. We believe such an 
application of these provisions of the Act assists us in fulfilling our obligation to liberally 
construe the Act to accomplish its purposes (see Section 81003). The revolving door provisions 
were added by legislation sponsored by the Commission in 1979. 
"That legislation, AB 1048 (Waters), was drafted by the Commission to 
embody the following principles: 
'1 The interests of the government and the public as a former client 
should be protected by preventing the disclosure or use of matters revealed by 
reason of a relationship having confidential aspects. 
'2 The integrity of the governmental decision making process should be 
protected by insuring avoidance of the appearance of impropriety.  
•3 Both the interests of the government and the public and the integrity of 
the governmental decision making process should be protected by limitations on 
the opportunity for undue advantage by reason of familiarity with agency practice 
and procedure and personal relationships with agency personnel." (In re Lucas, 
14 FPPC Ops. 14, 0-00-157 (2000).) 
Therefore, as an otherwise paid employee for similar types of services, we find Dr. Meral 
is representing his employer for compensation even when technically volunteering his services on 
behalf of his employer only while appearing before his former agency. For that reason, Dr. 
Meral may not "voluntarily" provide services on behalf of his employer that are otherwise 
prohibited under the Act's revolving door provisions while receiving compensation for other 
services.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: I) As the former Deputy Secretary of the California Natural Resources Agency, what 
restrictions do the Act's revolving door provisions have on Dr. Meral's private employment with 
the Natural Heritage Institute? 
2) Do the Act's revolving door provisions apply if Dr. Meral acts as an "unpaid 
volunteer" on behalf of his employer while simultaneously being compensated for any services 
that are not prohibited? 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. 2. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
wriuen advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

CONCLUSION: 1) Under the one-year ban, Dr. Meral is prohibited as a paid employee from making an 
appearance or communication before his former agency, or any agency under the direction and 
control of his former agency, to influence any administrative or legislative action and any action 
or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property for 12 months after leaving 
governmental employment. Dr. Mera] is also prohibited under the permanent ban from making 
an appearance or communication on behalf of his private employer, or from assisting his 
employer in making an appearance or communication before any state administrative agency for 
the purpose of influencing a proceeding involving a specific party or parties if he previously 
participated in the proceeding as a state official. 
2) For purposes of the revolving door provisions, Dr. Meral is representing his private 
employer for compensation so long as he is a paid employee. Dr. Meral may not provide 
"volunteer" services on behalf of his employer, which would otherwise be prohibited, while 
simultaneously being compensated for other services.

(See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (/11 re Oglesby (1975) l FPPC Ops. 71.) All statutory references arc to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 2.
